sentences.text: Good morning, Chana.
Of course, it's Flora Carasso Mihael.
F-L-O-R-A C-A-R-A-- double-S-O M-I-H-A-E-L.
I was born in Thessaloniki, Greece.
As it is today or--
It was a a very Jewish city.
Were 56,000 Jews living in Thessaloniki.
Very close-- the families were very, very close and united.
And Jews were in Salonika, because we called it Salonika, also Thessaloniki-- were there before the Greeks gave it to Greece, to Thessaloniki.
Until 1912, it was, as we can say, a Jewish city. 1912, the Greek army liberated the city from the Turks because the Turks had conquered Thessaloniki.
And the Jews started to strive on with their lives and build a healthy and strong community until the invasion of the Germans.
It was in 1935.
My family was a large family.
I remember living always with my aunts, uncles, grandmother, not having many friends outside of the family.
It was always-- the houses were built like villas, one near the other, and always playing and feeling the love and care of the family.
Not the friends, all family.
My father's name was Rene and my mother's Alien.
My father was representing a factory from Athens.
He used to send the products they use-- they used to produce soap, combs, and glue.
Of course, my family life changed.
We-- our houses were requisition-- requisitioned by the Germans.
I remember very well a German officer that lived in our house.
And I had the typhus then.
And when he learned from my mother, who spoke a little bit of German, that I had typhus, he washed his hands with alcohol and left.
And went he got drunk, he used to tell my mother, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
It means that all the Jews will be shot.
And I remember the family said, don't worry.
Don't listen to him.
He's drunk.
Nobody could believe.
And later on, my-- my grandmother crying, my mother also.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse-- excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she-- she used to tell me, Mother, don't worry about me.
We are going to work.
We're going to peel potatoes.
And we come back.
Because the-- the news were that the Jews would be sent to Madagascar to make a new state there, a Jewish state.
And they had also to pay their tickets, their train tickets.
But only my father, my mother, and me, and one of my aunts with her husband been were not in this plan because we had the Spanish and the Italian nationality.
And since the Italians were fighting with the Germans until a certain date, and the German-- and Spain with Franco was neutral, so we were free to walk on the streets.
We were not-- we had not to wear our yellow stars.
But all our family, my aunts, uncles, cousins, were-- had to wear their yellow stars until they were brought in a ghetto that was called Baron Hirsch.
Yes, I went to grammar school.
And sadly, I remember that my mother wouldn't let me go in a-- to school.
I don't remember if I was not allowed to go.
To tell you the truth, they didn't tell me.
It was a-- I was seven then.
I stopped going to school until after the war.
No, it was-- no, it was not a Jewish school.
It was a-- a Greek private school, one of the-- the best for girls.
At home, my parents and all my family spoke Ladino and French.
Greek was their third language, which they didn't learn.
Until my parents died, they made a lot of mistakes when they spoke in Greek.
Their-- their language was Ladino and French because when they went to school, there were no Greek schools.
There were only French schools.
After 1912, there were schools.
Already, my parents were grown-- were grownups.
Yes, both of them.
Both of them, all the family, all the family.
But we came from Spain.
There were a lot of Jewish organizations.
There were orphanages.
There were-- there was [NON-ENGLISH] where poor people would go out there for soup.
There was [NON-ENGLISH] It was a kind of [? vigil ?] before the war where the ladies used to knit and embroider and then sell all of these items in bazaars.
And with the money, they would help younger Jewish girls to-- and send them to farm schools in-- in Palestine then.
And there was the Maccabi also.
Many Jewish schools, many Jewish schools.
The first printing was done by a Jew in Thessaloniki.
We had a lot of new Greek newspapers written in Ladino, in French, and in Greek.
There were a lot of doctors, lawyers, businessman, intellectuals, artists, [INAUDIBLE].
And it was an active Jewish life.
Before the war, I can't tell you because I was very young, perhaps, to understand was-- was-- what was Palestine and Zionism.
But after the war, a lot of survivors left for Palestine.
And my father was a very good Zionist.
And living with him, he gave me-- he transmitted his love for Israel.
That's why I'm-- I try to work and I love Israel very much.
Before the war, no.
Before the war, no.
It was not a religious family.
It-- mine was a traditional family.
I don't remember myself before the war going to a synagogue.
I don't remember.
Because-- perhaps my mother took me.
But I don't remember.
After the war, of course, I didn't miss the holidays.
Not on Shabbat.
We didn't light candles at home on Shabbat.
But I went on Yom Ha'atzmaut, Yom Kippur, and all the other holidays, always until I do until today.
In 1939, my father didn't lose his job.
There was a lot of chit-chat in the family how they were going to save their-- their houses, their businesses, and not speaking about hiding, not at all.
They couldn't imagine what Germans' idea was about to the Final Solution.
They couldn't imagine that they were going to Germany or to Poland and they wouldn't come back.
And even though there were Greek people that would come and tell the young people of the family, come with us to the fight in the mountains, they wouldn't they their parents.
The Jewish family was very, very, very close.
And I think that that's why many, many young people died because they were-- they wouldn't let their parents, leave-- leave alone.
They said, we're going to Germany.
We work and come back.
Nobody had come back and tell us-- and I don't know if he had-- if someone had come back, if people could believe what happened.
We were-- our community was aware-- aware, because in July-- 7th of July, 1943, all the men from 16 were gathered in the square in Salonika, which is called until today liberation, Liberty Square.
And all the men were there.
They-- they-- they were kept under a burning sun for hours and hours.
Many fainted.
And then they took the Jewish to labor camps outside Thessaloniki.
They knew, of course, that something was wrong.
We had a Rabbi [?
Koretz, ?] who was not Greek.
He was from Austria.
And I don't know how he-- his attitude during the war is ambiguous.
It's not very clean.
And he tried to persuade Jews to stay put, not to leave, that everything would be all right.
The Germans would treat them fine.
And they would go to Poland and come back and find everything.
So there were very few Jews that ran away, that went to a mountain or went to-- not to Israel.
Where it was?
They-- they left Greece.
They-- they left Salonika for Athens and for the islands also because Jews were not only in Salonika.
The were in Athens, in the islands also.
And now, there are no communities anymore.
At what point after 1939 was your family affected?
What-- did your father lose his job at a certain point?
No, my father didn't lose his job.
He kept his job until we fled to Athens.
In Athens, no.
In Thessaloniki, there was a ghetto, Baron Hirsch.
It was called Baron Hirsch.
Must be '43.
Then the Jews were all around up there.
They had to write down their names on the list of the community.
And they were all sent in Baron Hirsch.
And that-- that was a ghetto near the railway station.
And from there, the Germans took a voice.
And they started sending them to Poland.
I have never visited the ghetto.
It was not possible for outsiders to go into the ghetto.
It must've been bigger now.
When I passed from there, my husband used to tell me that was the ghetto.
It's full of houses and buildings.
You can see nothing.
There isn't even the plaque telling that, you know, in 1943, Jews suffered here.
No, nothing.
It's just buildings and the railway station.
Exempt were only Jews with foreign nationality and the-- the community members, the council.
We it before the war, since my great-grandparents came from Spain.
But we had it before the war.
It was legal.
No, just my father, my father, my mother, and me, nobody else in the family.
I don't know how.
But we were the only ones.
Not in our own house.
We moved several times with other families because you were not allowed to have an big apartment for yourself.
And I remember, we moved with other-- with two other families, friends of my parents.
We moved together.
This was they started taking Jews away.
It must be about a year before we fled to Athens.
Must be about 1943.
But I-- I remember leaving freely, walking on the street with my parents, always with my parents, always.
I was free.
I didn't feel that there was restricted in anything.
Perhaps I was a child also and didn't feel it.
I didn't understand.
Plans of escape, they wouldn't talk in front of the children.
Talking about what-- what would become of their belongings, yes, I remember.
They-- they took these, the carpets in one warehouse.
For me, it was like a story.
I couldn't understand what they were talking about.
I didn't miss the carpets.
I didn't miss the-- the furniture.
Of course, I missed my grandmother and my aunts and uncles and especially one cousin.
This I felt very well.
But I stayed a child.
Since I was near my parents, nothing could hurt me.
That's what I believed.
It was a nice apartment in the center of the city.
We were able to take our belongings as much as we could because my father had already given a lot of our belongings to Greek families.
Or he would hid-- hide them in warehouses.
Sometimes by choice, sometimes no.
Sometimes no.
I remember very well, it happens that I was there because my father didn't tell-- I was there.
And I remember someone coming at home.
It was a table, a dinner table that I have it.
I have it at home.
It's very precious to me, for me.
And someone telling my father, you give it to me.
I'll keep it during the war.
And if you come back, I'll give it back to you.
And he did.
My father knew where it was.
And he went and took the table.
And I have it at home.
Some didn't.
But Greeks help a lot, especially young people take them to-- onto the mountains for the resistance.
There must have been.
Well, we had traitors in our community.
It's not some-- they tell me that you shouldn't say that.
Why?
We had traitor Jews, traitors in our community.
We had people that denounced other Jews.
And I don't know how they felt because perhaps it was for their survival.
And people-- these people-- those people came back from the camp.
And they were just and sentenced in Salonika.
And two were shot after the war.
Yes.
Yes.
In 56,000, you can find one or two or three or four.
I knew them because they were very, very good friends of my father.
I knew them because my mother used to take me on a visit there.
And I spent many evenings with them.
So I knew them.
Normal life with a lot of sadness, with a lot of fear, of course, with not [? knowing ?] what would become of us, [? we're ?] [? not about to get ?] tomorrow, and a lot of privation.
Yes, I've stopped school.
I don't remember asking why.
I was very, very attached and sad when I saw my cousin leave.
He was my dear cousin.
And that I felt it.
And I feel it even today.
And losing, of course, all-- all the aunts that loved me because I was the only girl in the family.
That was something.
And they didn't have many children.
There were two boys and a girl.
And my father tried to save my cousin, who was only six months old.
He went to the Spanish consulate and tried to persuade them to take the child as his own.
But he didn't succeed.
They wouldn't let him.
I remember all this fuss and all this talking, my father running and trying to save this baby.
His name was David.
He didn't succeed.
He would have grown up and perhaps he wouldn't have even known that he was not my brother.
Then my father had an employee who unfortunately collaborated.
And the day-- one day, he sees my father-- he meets my father on the street and tells him, Rene, are you still leaving Salonika?
And I remember my father coming home and said, Alien, we have to leave because he recognized my father.
And since he knew that he was not very clean, and he had collaborated with the Germans, he got frightened.
I don't-- I don't know how, but he succeeded in-- in fake-- having false identity cards as Christian Greeks.
My name was still Flora then.
The last name was [?
Salto. ?] I don't know how he chose-- chose this name.
And we took the [? tradotta. ?] The [? tradotta ?] was a train that took Italian soldiers to Athens.
And we went to Athens with these train with false identity card-- papers.
And we were received very warmly in a Greek Christian home.
It was my father's superior from the factory he used to work to.
And his grandson was decorated last year in June because our community decorated and made this special event for the Greeks that helped the Jews.
And-- and he came.
And he was decorated for the-- what his grandfather saved us.
I mean, we were on the street.
We didn't have where to live.
We didn't have any money.
And he took us in his house, the three of us.
He even found an apartment where we went to live.
And from there, we went to-- were sent to-- to Bergen-Belsen.
With our false identity cards.
My mother and father, yes.
I didn't feel anything.
It was for a trip for me.
Perhaps it was the first trip for me.
Our belongings were some suitcases.
My mother tried to put inside the satchel, especially food.
And the-- I remember that all the children and myself used to wear this back-- the bags you use now.
Everybody wears these bags.
They put it on the shoulder.
There were-- there was water and food and some cloth.
And of course, I didn't know that.
But my mother had sewn a ring with a diamond on my shoulder bag-- bag.
I had a red coat.
And she had sewn there a ring because you didn't know after the war or doing the war, you didn't have to sell jewelry to save yourself.
I didn't know that until much, much later.
Perhaps when I was engaged, because she gave me this ring.
And she said that it-- I had it on me even in the camp.
No, no.
He just called them from the railway station.
We didn't have anybody.
We didn't have any relatives in Athens.
So we had to phone call a name.
His name was Vasser Condira.
I remember this family very, very well.
He called.
And he said, Mr.
Vasser, I'm here.
Where are you?
At the train station.
I'm with that Alien and Flora.
And what are you doing?
I don't have where to go.
Can-- do you know a hotel?
No, you come home.
And that picture that is in front of me is an open door with all of the family standing outside waiting for us.
And did you live as Christians while you lived in Athens?
No, no as Jews, as Jews.
In Athens, it was not the Thessaloniki.
The Jews in Athens were few.
And the restrictions were not the same as in Thessaloniki.
They didn't have any ghetto.
No, we were free.
Yeah, we used them in Athens also.
But I remember going on promenade with my parents and going to the market.
But they tried not to speak Greek very much because they had-- their pronunciation, if Jews opened their-- their mouth, you would understand they were not Greeks.
No, there was no.
No, no, no, no.
In that house, we're about one month.
And then they found a-- another apartment near their home so they could be in touch.
But all day, we passed it with them.
All my father's questions and troubles, even our jewelry, Mr.
Condira put it in a vase with cotton.
And then he took two tiles from the-- his garden, sent all the family away, and he put the jewelry there.
And after the war, I have the picture that he takes off the tiles.
And he gave the vase with the jewelry to my father.
Yes, they were great people.
